title: Individual and city-level variations in heat-related road traffic deaths in Latin America

data_scope.note: per-subgroup sample sizes and the exact six-country list are not stated in the abstract/captions reviewed

core_conclusion: Across 272 cities in six Latin American countries (2000-2019, >1.9 million city-days), road traffic mortality risk rose monotonically with ambient temperature and was significantly elevated on extremely hot days (95th and 99th percentiles). The heat penalty fell disproportionately on younger people (<=19), males, motorcyclists and bicyclists, and on cities with hotter climates, longer commutes and more extended street segments.
why_it_matters: First-of-its-kind multi-city quantification linking extreme heat to road-traffic deaths in a fast-motorizing, highly urbanized region; resolves the burden by age, sex and road-user type; connects body-level vulnerability to urban form and commute exposure; centers peripheral, informal, non-climate-controlled transport in the tropical Global South.
why_nature: Multi-city, transferable dose-response evidence rather than a single case; couples climate, mobility and health at the space-human-health intersection; equity and Global-South framing; directly policy-translatable to transport and heat-adaptation planning.
added_value_to_my_research: Morphology-resolved street-level heat exposure surface from remote sensing -> explainable-ML crash-risk attribution
Mobility-data denominator (GPS/mobile-phone) -> demand-aware, heat-conditioned exposure metric reusing 2SFCA machinery
Commute / job-housing accessibility gradient -> intra-urban heat-mortality equity maps with a health endpoint
Shade/cooling/protection placement as efficiency-vs-equity facility-location optimization (reuse of EV-charging framework)
Physics-informed, transferable heat-mortality operator extrapolating to data-poor Global-South cities
